name: Amy Beliveau
background: Six-year resident, Town Meeting member for precinct seven, with over a decade of experience in development, design, negotiation, real estate, and development finance. She also cited service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee.
summary: Amy Beliveau won a Planning Board seat in 2026, defeating incumbent Nicholas Rossettos for one of two open seats. In campaign materials and a candidate forum, she emphasized balanced growth, housing production, data-driven decision-making, and using real estate and development expertise to help Winchester grow while respecting town history.
locationNote: Winchester resident and Town Meeting member representing precinct seven.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting and town committee service
priorRecordSummary: Beliveau cited service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee, along with private-sector development and real estate experience, rather than prior service on the Planning Board itself.

stanceCards.label: No school facilities position in provided sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources focus on planning, housing, zoning, and development, not school buildings or facilities.

stanceCards.label: Supports expanding housing options
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, she said Winchester should look beyond transit-oriented corridors and consider interior conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods, while arguing size alone does not determine historic value or housing opportunity.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes development finance and town leverage
stanceCards.detail: She said she understands project financials and what levers the town can use to create value and achieve its objectives, and described herself as a data-driven decision maker.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on Planning Board experience and approach
evidence.summary: Beliveau said Winchester should grow in a balanced and thoughtful way and highlighted more than a decade of experience in development, design, negotiation, real estate, and project finance, along with Town Meeting, EFPBC, and State of the Town Committee service.

evidence.title: Planning Board forum on housing growth and preservation
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Beliveau said she brings real estate industry knowledge the board lacks, argued that historic value is not tied to small size, and urged the town to consider interior conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods in addition to transit-oriented growth areas.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election results
evidence.summary: In the Planning Board race for two seats, Keri Layton received 2,331 votes, Amy S. Beliveau 2,042, and Nicholas J. Rossettos 1,864, electing Beliveau to one of the two seats.

evidence.title: Election-night report confirms Beliveau victory
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that Beliveau unseated incumbent Nicholas Rossettos to claim one of two open Planning Board seats.

name: Heather von Mering
background: Mother of five in Winchester Public Schools with experience across multiple school levels, including out-of-district placement. Works as a vice president in construction and asset planning, and has served 16 years in Winchester town government, including on the Historical Commission, Planning Board, Capital Planning Committee, as Town Moderator, and as a Town Meeting Member.
summary: Von Mering ran for School Committee in 2026 emphasizing parent experience, literacy reform, special education advocacy, facilities planning, and municipal finance knowledge. She argued for closing literacy and math gaps, supported the district’s literacy curriculum shift, and called for attention to student mental health while expressing caution about making technology policy without subject-matter expertise. She lost the race to John Bellaire.
priorRecordSummary: Her documented public record is mainly outside the School Committee itself: long service in town government and a 2025 Town Meeting role supporting the school budget as requested rather than using Circuit Breaker funds, while urging a longer-term financial plan for schools.

stanceCards.label: Backed literacy reform
stanceCards.detail: Described herself as a long-time literacy reform advocate, supported the district’s new literacy curriculum, and focused on early identification and support for dyslexia and language-based learning disabilities.

stanceCards.label: Called for mental health attention
stanceCards.detail: Campaign coverage said both candidates called for greater attention to student mental health.

stanceCards.label: Cautious on technology policy
stanceCards.detail: Said the School Committee should rely on outside expertise on artificial intelligence and that she was not the right person to write technology policy alone.

stanceCards.label: Facilities and capital planning background
stanceCards.detail: Highlighted professional experience in construction and asset planning as informing her approach to facilities governance and stewardship of school buildings.

stanceCards.label: Supported requested school budget
stanceCards.detail: At 2025 Town Meeting, she said Town Meeting should fund the school budget as requested and proposed adding free cash to offset the deficit while opposing use of Circuit Breaker funds for the FY26 budget.

evidence.title: Candidate profile cites parent, advocacy, professional and town-government experience
evidence.summary: Von Mering said she would bring perspectives as a parent of five in the schools, a literacy and special education advocate, a construction and asset-planning professional, and a longtime town leader with municipal finance experience.

evidence.title: Forum coverage on literacy and special education
evidence.summary: At the School Committee forum, she supported the new literacy curriculum, discussed dyslexia identification failures, and argued that early intervention improves outcomes and lowers later support costs.

evidence.title: Forum coverage on technology policy
evidence.summary: She said the School Committee should rely on outside expertise on AI and that writing technology policy without expertise would be inappropriate.

evidence.title: Election-night recap of campaign message
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said von Mering argued the district should close literacy and math gaps before taking on new commitments, and that both candidates called for greater attention to student mental health.

evidence.title: 2025 Town Meeting support for school budget request
evidence.summary: At Town Meeting, von Mering said she did not support using Circuit Breaker funds for the FY26 budget, supported funding the school budget as requested, and asked for a financial plan to continue supporting the schools.

evidence.title: 2026 School Committee election result
evidence.summary: Official results show John H. Bellaire defeated Heather R. von Mering, 2,192 votes to 1,861.

name: John Bellaire
background: Winchester High School graduate who studied education and public policy at Brown University and works for Bellwether, a national education nonprofit focused on state school finance. In his candidate profile, he described himself as an education researcher, youth mentor, former substitute teacher at Vinson-Owen Elementary, and Weekend Mentor for Winchester A Better Chance.
summary: Bellaire presented himself as a young education-policy candidate with local school experience and a focus on student opportunity. Across the available sources, he emphasized school finance, literacy improvement, classroom technology, and student mental health.
locationNote: Winchester Public Schools graduate; the forum coverage says he graduated from Winchester High School in 2021.
priorRecordLabel: Education policy and local youth work
priorRecordSummary: The sources show professional experience in education research and school finance, plus youth mentoring and substitute teaching, but do not show a prior elected record on the School Committee.

stanceCards.label: Budget and finance focus
stanceCards.detail: He highlighted his work developing financial models for equitable and transparent school finance systems and discussed special education spending in the district budget.

stanceCards.label: Push for stronger literacy work
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, he said Winchester had been delayed on literacy and pointed to declining proficiency data, arguing for earlier support so students do not fall behind.

stanceCards.label: Support student mental health
stanceCards.detail: In his profile, he said his student experience motivates him to support student mental health.

stanceCards.label: Attention to classroom technology
stanceCards.detail: In his profile, he said he wants to address technology in the classroom.

evidence.title: Candidate profile describes education research and mentoring background
evidence.summary: Bellaire said he works in education research and school finance, conducts school quality reviews, mentors students through Winchester A Better Chance, worked as a substitute teacher, and is a Winchester Public Schools graduate.

evidence.title: Candidate profile names campaign motivations
evidence.summary: He wrote that his experience as a Winchester student motivates him to provide high-quality student engagement opportunities, address technology in the classroom, and support student mental health.

evidence.title: Forum coverage highlights literacy and budget comments
evidence.summary: At the candidate forum, Bellaire said Winchester had been 'behind the curve and a bit delayed' on literacy, cited MCAS reading trends, and argued that earlier intervention could help students before they fall further behind; he also referenced special education's share of the recommended school budget.

evidence.title: Forum statement on student opportunity
evidence.summary: Bellaire said every student should have the opportunity to develop into their best selves and that he wants to support students in achieving the futures they envision for themselves.

evidence.title: 2026 town election result
evidence.summary: Official results show John H. Bellaire received 2,192 votes in the School Committee race, ahead of Heather R. Von Mering's 1,861, for the one seat to be elected.

name: Keri Layton
background: Incumbent Planning Board member seeking a second term in 2026. Layton previously served as a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member, was appointed to the Master Plan Steering Committee, and later served three years on the Conservation Commission, including during review of the 10 Converse Place project. She also chaired the Planning Board during North Main Street rezoning work.
summary: Layton ran as an incumbent with experience in town planning, conservation, and master-plan work. Her campaign emphasized balancing housing growth with environmental and design concerns, implementing zoning reforms such as MBTA Communities compliance and ADUs, and using mixed-use development to broaden the commercial tax base.
locationNote: Winchester; previously a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member and later elected as a Precinct 3 Town Meeting member in the 2026 results.
priorRecordLabel: Planning Board incumbent and former Conservation Commission member
priorRecordSummary: Layton cited service on the Planning Board, including chairing the board during North Main Street rezoning, work advancing the Main Street Mixed Use District and ADU bylaw changes, and participation in review of 10 Converse Place/33 Mount Vernon Street. She also served on the Conservation Commission and Master Plan Steering Committee.

stanceCards.label: No clear schools facilities platform in sources
stanceCards.detail: Sources do not show a specific position on school buildings or facilities.

stanceCards.label: Mixed-use development to expand tax base
stanceCards.detail: Layton said mixed-use projects can expand the commercial tax base without adding students to schools.

stanceCards.label: Supports adding housing and affordable units
stanceCards.detail: She argued Winchester must add housing, highlighted the town's subsidized-housing shortfall, and discussed setting affordable housing expectations early in development review.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes clearer expectations in development review
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, Layton said the board should use pre-application meetings to clarify affordable housing requirements, aesthetics, and height expectations for developers.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines town and board experience
evidence.summary: Layton said she began as a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member in 2017, served on the Master Plan Steering Committee, then served three years on the Conservation Commission before joining the Planning Board. She said she ran to improve how Planning Board work balances Conservation Commission interests.

evidence.title: Profile cites zoning work on MBTA Communities, MSMD, and ADUs
evidence.summary: Layton said she chaired the board during the bylaw change implementing the MBTA 3A Communities Act, helped bring the Main Street Mixed Use District to Town Meeting, and supported ADUs by right.

evidence.title: Forum comments on housing production and subsidized housing gap
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Layton said Winchester was still roughly 400 units short of the state-required subsidized housing threshold and suggested steady production over time could close the gap.

evidence.title: Forum comments on spreading housing growth and setting developer expectations
evidence.summary: Layton urged creative thinking about distributing housing across town and said pre-application meetings should clearly state affordable housing requirements, desired aesthetics, and height limits.

evidence.title: Forum comments on fiscal impact of mixed-use projects
evidence.summary: Layton said mixed-use projects can expand the commercial tax base without adding students to schools.

evidence.title: 2026 election results: Layton wins Planning Board seat
evidence.summary: Official results show Keri Layton received 2,331 votes in the two-seat Planning Board race, finishing first and winning reelection.

name: Michelle Prior
background: Incumbent Select Board member and chair seeking a second term in 2026. Prior said she had served 15 years in Town Meeting for Precinct 4, six years on the Finance Committee including as FY24 chair, three years on the Select Board including two years as chair, and on the Audit Advisory Committee and Retiree Health Insurance Advisory Committee. She also said she served five years on the Wright-Locke Farm Conservancy board.
summary: Prior ran as an experienced incumbent focused on long-term financial planning, non-property-tax revenue, cross-board coordination, and housing options for residents across ages and incomes. In override debates, she argued for asking voters only for funding the board judged necessary and emphasized flexibility in how funds could be used.
locationNote: Winchester townwide race for Select Board.
priorRecordLabel: Incumbent Select Board chair; longtime town governance record
priorRecordSummary: Sources describe Prior as Select Board chair in 2025-26 and document her stated prior service in Town Meeting, Finance Committee, and town advisory roles.

stanceCards.label: Pursues non-property-tax revenue
stanceCards.detail: Called for more revenue beyond residential property taxes through commercial growth, fee reviews, outside-hauler transfer station access, and leasing town assets such as roof space for solar.

stanceCards.label: Frames housing as a way to keep Winchester accessible
stanceCards.detail: Said the town should support housing development that serves different family configurations and gives seniors options to remain in Winchester and age in place.

stanceCards.label: Favored a narrower override ask
stanceCards.detail: During override negotiations, she argued against asking voters for more than modeling showed was needed and said she preferred not to seek extra money 'just in case.'

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes process, planning, and coordination
stanceCards.detail: Ran on long-term financial planning, support for the new town manager, and better communication across boards and committees.

stanceCards.label: Override package included school facilities funding
stanceCards.detail: The February 2026 override agreement included capital funding partly for school facilities, though the sources do not show a detailed school-facilities platform beyond that broader package.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines experience and priorities
evidence.summary: Prior described her service on Town Meeting, Finance Committee, Select Board, and town advisory committees, and highlighted revenue diversification, housing, and communication as top issues.

evidence.title: Official minutes list Prior as Select Board chair
evidence.summary: May 30, 2025 joint meeting minutes identify Michelle Prior as Chair of the Select Board at a State of the Town Committee meeting.

evidence.title: Prior led non-property-tax revenue workstream
evidence.summary: In her candidate profile, Prior said she led State of the Town work on non-property-tax revenue, researched fee comparisons, and expanded transfer station access for outside haulers at higher rates.

evidence.title: Prior argued for a limited override ask
evidence.summary: At the February 2026 override negotiations, Prior said the board should not ask voters for money beyond what modeling showed was necessary and argued for maintaining flexibility rather than tying funds to a narrow stabilization purpose.

evidence.title: After override loss, Prior urged more public feedback
evidence.summary: Following the ballot defeat, Prior said the board should learn why the override failed through focus groups, listening sessions, and surveys before moving ahead, while expecting a new ballot question within the year.

evidence.title: Prior won re-election to Select Board
evidence.summary: Official results show Michelle I. Prior defeated Shamus M. Brady 2,849 to 1,159 in the 2026 Select Board race.

name: Nick Rossettos
background: Incumbent Planning Board member from Winchester. He said he has spent the past four years working on mixed-use development, helped approve the Waterfield affordable-housing mixed-use project, worked on zoning tied to MBTA 3A compliance and North Main Street, and focused on design standards. He also described himself as a fractional CFO for life sciences companies with a longstanding interest in Winchester’s historic design.
summary: Rossettos ran for reelection to the Planning Board in 2026 emphasizing housing production through mixed-use and inclusionary zoning while stressing design review, historic character, and fiscal balance between residential and commercial development.
locationNote: Grew up in Winchester and graduated from Winchester High School, according to his candidate profile.
priorRecordSummary: Rossettos described four years of Planning Board work on mixed-use development, the Waterfield approval, MBTA 3A-related zoning, North Main Street zoning, and design standards. A forum report also identified him as an incumbent and as liaison to the Design Review Committee and Historical Commission.
keyThemes: Housing growth with preservation of town character

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable and inclusionary housing
stanceCards.detail: He highlighted the unanimously approved Waterfield affordable-housing mixed-use development and said North Main Street zoning prioritizes inclusionary housing.

stanceCards.label: No clear schools platform in sources
stanceCards.detail: The available sources focus on planning, zoning, housing, and fiscal impacts rather than school facilities.

stanceCards.label: Wants development that expands tax base
stanceCards.detail: He said zoning should encourage mixed-use development to increase the town’s tax base and argued the town needs a mix of development types rather than only residential projects.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes financial discipline and balanced development
stanceCards.detail: Rossettos cited his CFO background and argued that profitable residential development should be balanced with other uses because 'the town needs a combination.'

evidence.title: Candidate profile on planning experience and priorities
evidence.summary: Rossettos said he worked over four years on mixed-use development, the Waterfield affordable-housing approval, MBTA 3A and North Main Street zoning, and design standards prioritizing inclusionary housing, density, and tax-base growth.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on growth and preservation
evidence.summary: At the Planning Board candidate forum, Rossettos said growth and preservation are compatible but require experience and judgment, and he centered his campaign on preserving downtown character.

evidence.title: Forum discussion of housing production and design
evidence.summary: He cited Waterfield, Cambridge Street, Washington and Swanton, and 10 Converse Place as examples tied to affordable housing and inclusionary units, and suggested creative housing forms such as multi-unit homes resembling historic single-family houses.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on fiscal impacts of development
evidence.summary: Rossettos said developers favor residential projects because they are profitable, but argued the town needs a combination of development types. He also said some zoning rules are complicated and confusing.

evidence.title: 2026 Planning Board election result
evidence.summary: In the 2026 town election for two Planning Board seats, Rossettos received 1,864 votes, behind Keri Layton (2,331) and Amy S. Beliveau (2,042), so he was not elected.

name: Shamus Brady
background: Former Winchester School Committee member (2021-2024), former Finance Committee member during the early COVID-19 period, and a public-school administrator/educator. In 2026 he was identified as director of special education for Everett Public Schools.
summary: Brady ran for Select Board in 2026 emphasizing school-budget knowledge, support for an operating override, affordability relief, stronger business revenue efforts, and a more openly progressive voice on the board. He lost to incumbent Michelle Prior.
locationNote: Winchester resident since 2014 and father of two children in public school, according to his candidate profile.
priorRecordLabel: School Committee and Finance Committee record
priorRecordSummary: Brady cited service on the Winchester School Committee from 2021-2024 and on the Finance Committee during the early months of COVID-19. Coverage from 2024 also documents him speaking and voting as a School Committee member on budget and literacy-related matters.

stanceCards.label: Deep school-budget focus
stanceCards.detail: He said his professional work and School Committee service gave him detailed knowledge of school budgeting and public finance.

stanceCards.label: Backed operating override
stanceCards.detail: He said Winchester needed to pass an operating override to maintain current service levels and that he fully supported it.

stanceCards.label: Affordability committee proposal
stanceCards.detail: He proposed a committee on affordability and pointed to tools such as the residential tax exemption as possible relief for impacted property owners.

stanceCards.label: Wants more business revenue
stanceCards.detail: He said the town should more actively recruit businesses and identify business types that fit Winchester while generating revenue.

stanceCards.label: Public process on cuts
stanceCards.detail: If an override failed, he said he would ask department heads to identify essential services and possible cuts, then bring proposals to the public through feedback and forums.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines experience and priorities
evidence.summary: Brady described his School Committee and Finance Committee experience, backed an operating override, proposed affordability measures, and called for business recruitment and public input on any cuts.

evidence.title: Supported additional school budget initiatives
evidence.summary: As a School Committee member, Brady voiced support for proposals serving students directly and for preserving funding flexibility related to literacy curriculum work; the committee then voted unanimously for the budget increase.

evidence.title: Asked literacy access question during School Committee discussion
evidence.summary: At a 2024 School Committee meeting, Brady asked whether UFLI was available to all K-2 teachers in all schools, showing engagement on district literacy implementation.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage describes campaign message
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said Brady pushed for urgency, visible dissent and new revenue streams during the campaign.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election result
evidence.summary: Official results show Brady received 1,159 votes in the Select Board race, behind Michelle Prior with 2,849 votes.

name: Anthea Brady
background: Select Board incumbent seeking re-election in 2025. Brady said she has lived in Winchester for 11 years, holds a Master of Public Policy, previously worked in the education sector with federal, state and local agencies, and currently works with organizations on culture and compliance. She said she had served three years on the Select Board, including two as vice chair, and had also served on the Personnel Board, represented the board on the Field Management Committee, and was a Precinct 1 Town Meeting member.
summary: Incumbent Select Board member re-elected in 2025. Brady emphasized town fiscal planning, possible use of free cash or an operating override to address the structural deficit, continuity in capital planning, support for schools, and economic development and housing growth.
locationNote: Lives in the Muraco neighborhood, according to her 2025 candidate profile.
priorRecordSummary: Brady said she had served three years on the Select Board and was vice chair for two. Official May 2025 meeting minutes list her as Select Board vice-chair. Coverage in 2025 and 2026 also describes her speaking and negotiating as a sitting board member on school funding and override questions.
keyThemes: Town fiscal planning and structural deficit
Support for schools and school funding
Economic development and commercial tax base

stanceCards.label: Supports maintaining school funding
stanceCards.detail: Brady said the school system is a critical part of town infrastructure and should be supported with high-quality teachers, curriculum, professional development and classroom supports.

stanceCards.label: Open to an override
stanceCards.detail: In her profile and at the 2025 candidate forum, Brady said free cash could help in the short run but an override might be necessary to address the town's structural deficit.

stanceCards.label: Wants broader revenue growth
stanceCards.detail: She pointed to mixed-use development, reopening MBTA station access and zoning opportunities as ways to expand commercial activity and the tax base.

stanceCards.label: Backs capital and playground investments
stanceCards.detail: Brady cited support for playground safety work and funding, and at the forum stressed continuity around a capital plan.

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable housing growth
stanceCards.detail: She said Winchester should advance both large and small scale affordable housing and work with the Affordable Housing Trust on 160 Forest Street.

evidence.title: 2025 candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Brady described her education and work background, said she was seeking re-election after three years on the Select Board, and called for maintaining essential services while considering free cash or an override. She also discussed affordable housing and commercial development.

evidence.title: Candidate forum highlights budget and capital planning views
evidence.summary: At the March 2025 League of Women Voters debate, Brady said the budget was critical, said free cash could help in the short run while an override might be necessary, and emphasized continuity around a capital plan.

evidence.title: Re-elected to Select Board in 2025
evidence.summary: Official election results show Brady won one of two Select Board seats with 1,649 votes, behind Paras Bhayani and ahead of Dorothy Simboli and Richard Welch Jr.

evidence.title: Election-night reporting confirms 2025 Select Board result
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that Paras Bhayani and incumbent Anthea Brady were the winners in the 2025 Select Board race.

evidence.title: Spoke in support of amended school budget
evidence.summary: At Town Meeting coverage in May 2025, Brady spoke in support of the amended school budget and said the schools obviously need help with math and that it was time for the town to make an investment now.

evidence.title: Official minutes list Brady as vice-chair after re-election
evidence.summary: Minutes from the May 30, 2025 joint School Committee and Select Board State of the Town meeting list Anthea Brady as Select Board vice-chair.

name: Christian Nixon
background: Architect with two degrees from Rice University and an education policy fellowship from Northeastern University’s Institute for Educational Leadership. He said he oversees budgeting, design, and construction of capital projects for a nearby community and school district, with more than 25 years of K-12 education and healthcare project experience. Nixon also described extensive Winchester service, including Town Meeting, School Committee, Capital Planning Committee, Audit Advisory Committee, and facilities-related working groups.
summary: Incumbent School Committee member who ran on school funding, long-term town revenue planning, facilities projects, and labor/organizational issues. He lost in the 2025 School Committee race.
locationNote: Winchester resident for 26 years, according to his candidate profile.
priorRecordSummary: Nixon’s profile says he has served on the School Committee since 2010 and on several other town finance and facilities bodies, giving him a direct record in school governance and capital planning.

stanceCards.label: Advocated for FY26 funding needs
stanceCards.detail: He listed making the case for Winchester Public Schools’ true FY26 funding needs as a top priority and pointed to use of the special education reserve fund to protect educational quality amid unplanned FY25 costs.

stanceCards.label: Backed sustainable revenue planning
stanceCards.detail: He said a top priority was supporting the town’s goals toward sustainable revenue models for long-term municipal and district needs.

stanceCards.label: Focused on facilities projects
stanceCards.detail: He highlighted the New Muraco statement of interest, Phase II of the 10-year facilities master plan, and funding for Lincoln Elementary improvements including HVAC and sitework.

stanceCards.label: Emphasized reserves and board coordination
stanceCards.detail: He described the special education reserve fund as a budget management tool with Select Board concurrence serving as a check-and-balance system.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and town service
evidence.summary: Nixon said he has lived in Winchester 26 years, has architecture and education policy credentials, and has served on the School Committee since 2010 along with other town committees.

evidence.title: Candidate profile lists priorities
evidence.summary: He identified FY26 school funding, sustainable revenue, Muraco and other facilities work, Lincoln Elementary improvements, and addressing WEA concerns as top priorities.

evidence.title: Candidate cites special education reserve fund use
evidence.summary: He said the School Committee approved use of more than $360,000 from the special education reserve fund to cover unbudgeted FY25 special education costs and protect the rest of the budget.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results
evidence.summary: In the School Committee race, Nixon received 975 votes, behind Stefanie Mnayarji and Karen Bolognese, who won the two seats.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage identifies outcome
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that the School Committee winners were Stefanie Mnayarji and incumbent Karen Maruyama Bolognese, while incumbent Christian Nixon received 975 votes.

name: Dorothy R. Simboli
background: Lawyer with commercial and residential real estate experience; Massachusetts Bar member since 1994 and Pennsylvania Bar member since 1992. Winchester resident for 25 years. Served on the Winchester Zoning Board of Appeals, including as chair since March 2024, and as a Town Meeting member since 2016.
summary: Select Board candidate focused on housing growth and historic preservation, support for updated school curriculum and student services, and tighter fiscal planning amid the town’s structural deficit. She also emphasized business-friendly permitting and commercial development.
locationNote: Winchester resident; Town Meeting member from Precinct 6 and Zoning Board of Appeals chair.
priorRecordLabel: Zoning Board of Appeals chair and Town Meeting member
priorRecordSummary: Simboli’s public record in the source packet comes mainly from appointed land-use service and Town Meeting participation rather than prior service on the Select Board or School Committee.
keyThemes: Updated curriculum including literacy and math
Capital planning for school and town facilities

stanceCards.label: Scrutiny of school spending
stanceCards.detail: Said the school budget warrants a deeper line-by-line review, including special education costs, while maintaining essential services.

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy improvement
stanceCards.detail: Called for updated curriculum and cited literacy as a critical issue; later asked how the district would measure success for the literacy pilot program.

stanceCards.label: Planning for major school projects
stanceCards.detail: Framed Lynch and the future Muraco project as part of a deferred capital backlog requiring sound fiscal planning.

stanceCards.label: Open to a right-sized override
stanceCards.detail: Said the town should consider an override, but only if it is strategically planned and appropriately sized.

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable housing growth
stanceCards.detail: Backed collaborative, manageably sized affordable housing projects and described 160 Forest Street as an opportunity for affordable housing while preserving neighborhood character.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Simboli described her legal and real-estate background, ZBA and Town Meeting service, and priorities including education, housing, commercial development, and fiscal stability.

evidence.title: Profile calls for detailed budget review
evidence.summary: She said the town’s structural deficit requires expert budget evaluation, suggested rebidding health insurance, and said the school budget and SPED costs merit a deep dive.

evidence.title: Profile supports affordable housing and business-friendly permitting
evidence.summary: She advocated collaborative affordable housing development, highlighted 160 Forest Street, and proposed streamlining permitting and studying parking options to expand the commercial tax base.

evidence.title: Select Board forum comments on override and planning
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Simboli said the town should consider a right-sized override and wanted earlier involvement in the budget process; she also referenced capital planning and possible use of EDA and CPA resources.

evidence.title: Town Meeting question on literacy pilot accountability
evidence.summary: During Town Meeting debate on the literacy pilot, Simboli asked how the School Department would determine success after the program ended.

evidence.title: 2025 election result
evidence.summary: In the four-candidate race for two Select Board seats, Simboli received 806 votes and did not win a seat.

name: Jack LeMenager
background: Winchester resident since 1988; served on the Historical Commission since 2009 and as its chair for the past eight years; 15-year Town Meeting member; member of the executive board of the Town Meeting Members Association; served on committees including the train station study committee and Community Preservation Act Advisory Committee; recently retired after work in management consulting and employee communications.
summary: Write-in candidate for Planning Board who argued the town should encourage more commercial and residential mixed-use development while improving coordination among boards and preserving Winchester’s architectural character. He won the open seat in the 2025 election.
locationNote: Focuses on Winchester development issues including North Main, the Center Business District, Converse Place, 654 Main St., and the Waterfield Lot.
priorRecordLabel: Historical Commission and Town Meeting service
priorRecordSummary: LeMenager’s cited public record is in related town governance and preservation roles rather than on the Planning Board itself, including long service on the Historical Commission, Town Meeting, and several town committees.

stanceCards.label: Better coordination across boards
stanceCards.detail: He said boards and commissions should work together more closely and that he would prioritize more communication and cross-fertilization of ideas, especially with the Select Board.

stanceCards.label: No school facilities position in sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources do not give a position on school facilities.

stanceCards.label: Encourage commercial development
stanceCards.detail: He said it is critical to encourage more commercial and residential development and specifically called for more commercial space in areas such as North Main.

stanceCards.label: Support for more residential development
stanceCards.detail: He supported greater mixed-use and residential development, but the sources do not provide a detailed housing affordability platform.

evidence.title: Campaign profile outlines development and coordination priorities
evidence.summary: LeMenager said Winchester should encourage more commercial and residential development, simplify the approval track where possible, and improve coordination among boards while protecting architectural integrity.

evidence.title: Background in Historical Commission and Town Meeting
evidence.summary: The profile says he has served on the Historical Commission since 2009, chaired it for eight years, served 15 years as a Town Meeting member, and participated in several town committees.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results for Planning Board
evidence.summary: Official results show Jack LeMenager received 739 votes in the Planning Board race, ahead of other write-in totals listed.

evidence.title: Election-night report confirms Planning Board win
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that LeMenager won the open Planning Board seat with 739 write-in votes.

name: Karen Maruyama Bolognese
background: Incumbent School Committee member first elected in 2019; chair in 2024-25 and 2021-22. Also a Town Meeting member since 2012, with additional town working-group and committee service. Professional background includes veterinary medicine, finance, translation, and teaching; degrees from Tufts, Michigan Ross, and Vanderbilt.
summary: Karen Maruyama Bolognese ran for reelection to School Committee in 2025 as an incumbent emphasizing long-term planning, fiscal sustainability, and support for district priorities across academics, arts, sports, and special education. She backed the superintendent-led literacy review process and described budgeting as a strategic, multi-year effort tied to Winchester 2030.
locationNote: Has lived in Winchester for 14 years.
priorRecordLabel: Incumbent School Committee chair and member
priorRecordSummary: Maruyama Bolognese served on the School Committee since 2019 and was chair in 2024-25 and 2021-22. In March 2025, local forum coverage described her as an incumbent candidate, and May 2025 town minutes list her as School Committee chair.

stanceCards.label: Long-term budget planning
stanceCards.detail: She said she has advocated a long-term, planful approach to budgeting and wants the five-year Winchester 2030 strategic plan and budget to guide sustainable growth and district priorities.

stanceCards.label: Tax burden awareness
stanceCards.detail: She said she takes fiscal responsibility seriously and recognizes the tax burden on residents while seeking to keep schools financially sustainable over the long term.

stanceCards.label: Supports superintendent-led literacy review
stanceCards.detail: She said she fully supports the superintendent's literacy approach, with educators and specialists reviewing materials, assessments, and professional development before bringing recommendations to the School Committee.

stanceCards.label: Education and family partnership on devices
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, she said phones and smartwatches can be distracting but argued a total ban on electronic devices would be difficult and emphasized wise usage and partnership with families.

stanceCards.label: Collaborative budget governance
stanceCards.detail: She described working with the superintendent, district leadership, the School Committee, and community stakeholders to prioritize staffing, programming, professional development, and facilities.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on background and priorities
evidence.summary: Profile lists her professional background, School Committee and Town Meeting service, and states her campaign emphasis on serving diverse student needs, long-term budgeting, and fiscal sustainability.

evidence.title: Literacy position in candidate Q&A
evidence.summary: She said she fully supports the superintendent's literacy approach, centered on educators and specialists piloting options in 2025-26 before School Committee review and action.

evidence.title: Forum comments on device policy
evidence.summary: Forum coverage says she viewed phones as a distraction but said banning all electronic devices would be difficult, emphasizing education about wise usage and partnership with families.

evidence.title: 2025 official election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Karen Bolognese received 1,512 votes in the School Committee race, one of two winning totals.

evidence.title: May 2025 School Committee chair listing
evidence.summary: State of the Town meeting minutes list Karen Maruyama Bolognese as School Committee chair on May 30, 2025.

evidence.title: Election-night reporting confirms winners
evidence.summary: Local election coverage reports that Stefanie Mnayarji and incumbent Karen Maruyama Bolognese won the two School Committee seats, while Christian Nixon lost.

name: Paras Bhayani
background: Corporate finance and business strategy professional with prior public-sector budget experience, including leading a $1.7 billion operating and capital budget for the Chicago Transit Authority. Winchester resident for five years and founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change.
summary: Bhayani ran for Select Board in 2025 emphasizing fiscal stewardship, long-term capital planning, housing infill, economic development, and climate/sustainability. His profile and forum remarks centered on balancing efficiencies with new revenue, including likely additional tax revenue to support town and school needs. He won one of two Select Board seats.
locationNote: Lives in Winchester with his wife and two daughters; said he had lived in town for five years as of February 2025.
priorRecordLabel: Education advocacy and finance background; later seated on Select Board
priorRecordSummary: Before election, Bhayani did not list prior elected or appointed Winchester town office in the profile source, but cited education advocacy as a founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change. By May 30, 2025, official meeting minutes list him as a Select Board member.
keyThemes: Fiscal stewardship and balanced town/school funding
Economic development and small business support

stanceCards.label: Balanced funding for town and schools
stanceCards.detail: Said the town faces a challenging budget situation and would need a mix of efficiencies and new revenue to protect fiscal health and meet town and WPS operating and capital needs.

stanceCards.label: Likely additional override revenue
stanceCards.detail: Said the 2019 override had outlasted its intended duration and that additional revenue would likely be needed; at the candidate forum he said taxpayers would likely need to provide additional money after efficiencies are identified.

stanceCards.label: Long-term capital planning
stanceCards.detail: Called for a five-year capital plan and referenced an override dedicated for capital projects; school facility needs including Muraco were part of the broader budget debate context.

stanceCards.label: Early literacy involvement
stanceCards.detail: Highlighted his role as a founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change, a parent-led group focused on strengthening early literacy curriculum in Winchester Public Schools.

stanceCards.label: Use technology for efficiency
stanceCards.detail: Proposed off-the-shelf technology to automate town processes and digitize approvals, permits, applications, and other processes.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines finance background and priorities
evidence.summary: Bhayani described his finance and public-sector budget background and said his candidacy focused on balanced town and school funding, capital projects, housing, economic development, and sustainability.

evidence.title: Candidate profile backs efficiencies plus new revenue
evidence.summary: He said Winchester would need carefully considered efficiencies and new revenue, and that additional revenue would likely be needed after the 2019 override outlasted its planned duration.

evidence.title: Candidate forum: likely need to go to taxpayers after efficiencies
evidence.summary: At the March 5 forum, Bhayani said the town needed a comprehensive plan to balance services and costs, could identify efficiencies, and then would likely need to seek additional taxpayer funding.

evidence.title: Profile highlights early literacy advocacy
evidence.summary: He said he was a founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change, focused on strengthening the district's early literacy curriculum.

evidence.title: Profile proposes technology and process modernization
evidence.summary: He proposed using off-the-shelf technology to automate processes and digitize permits, applications, approvals, and related town functions.

evidence.title: Profile supports infill and affordable housing approaches
evidence.summary: He said he favored thoughtful infill development, supported projects on already developed land near transit, and described 160 Forest Street as a possible model for small-scale affordable housing through renovation and an ADU.

name: Rick Welch
background: Winchester resident for 35 years. Real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including permitting, developing, and building multiple 40B projects across the state. He said he has served in the past decade as president of Mesiti Development Corporation, developing senior living units and working with town officials and boards.
summary: Welch ran for Select Board in 2025 emphasizing real estate development experience, affordable housing, senior housing, and ideas for expanding the commercial tax base. He said the town should address the structural deficit without cutting school or sports programs. He lost the 2025 election.
locationNote: Lives in Winchester and said his three children graduated from Winchester Public Schools.
priorRecordSummary: Welch presented professional experience in affordable housing, 40B projects, senior living development, and working with municipal permitting and boards, but the sources do not show prior elected service in Winchester government.

stanceCards.label: Supports more affordable and senior housing
stanceCards.detail: He said his housing-development experience could help the town address affordable housing needs and that he wants 160 Forest Street to offer opportunities for Winchester seniors struggling to remain in town.

stanceCards.label: Wants to expand the tax base through development
stanceCards.detail: He proposed more ambitious mixed-use downtown development and said 55-plus projects can bring in tax dollars.

stanceCards.label: Protect school and sports programs
stanceCards.detail: He said the town should find ways to address the deficit without touching school or sports programs and described school spending as money well spent.

stanceCards.label: Concerned about structural deficit
stanceCards.detail: He called the increased budget deficit concerning and said the town needs to look closely at ways to make up the gap while maintaining current standards.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines development background
evidence.summary: Welch said he is a real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including 40B projects and senior living development, and that he has worked with town officials and boards.

evidence.title: Says deficit should be addressed without cutting school or sports
evidence.summary: Welch said the growing budget deficit is concerning, that school money is well spent, and that the town should find ways to close the gap without touching school or sports programs.

evidence.title: Affordable housing and 160 Forest Street comments
evidence.summary: Welch said his experience would help the town navigate housing needs and that he would like 160 Forest Street to offer opportunities for Winchester seniors.

evidence.title: Proposes mixed-use growth and 55-plus incentives
evidence.summary: Welch said downtown expansion could include lower-level commercial space with upper-floor retail and residential uses, fewer parking requirements in some cases, and incentives for 55-plus projects to grow the tax base.

evidence.title: 2025 Select Board election result
evidence.summary: Official results show Richard Welch, Jr received 674 votes in the Select Board race. Two candidates were elected, and Welch was not among the top two vote-getters.

name: Stefanie Mnayarji
background: Economist with education at the London School of Economics and Northeastern University; worked in FinTech consulting and later founded a Boston-based company. Has lived in Winchester for eight years.
summary: First-time School Committee candidate in 2025 who emphasized literacy reform, broader curriculum review, more intentional technology use, and a school culture where every child thrives. She was elected to one of two School Committee seats in March 2025.
locationNote: Lives in Winchester and said she would bring needed elementary school representation.
priorRecordSummary: Previously elected as a Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member and cited service with the Parents' Inter-School Council, TMMA Board, Ambrose School Council, and Ambrose PTO. After the election, official minutes list her as a School Committee member and Recording Secretary.

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy reform
stanceCards.detail: Identified literacy reform as a primary reason for running and backed classroom investments and coaching to support curriculum rollout.

stanceCards.label: Wants tighter device limits
stanceCards.detail: Called for intentional use of technology, review of personal device policies, and supported a phone-free or device-free school experience.

stanceCards.label: Protect classroom spending
stanceCards.detail: Said budgets should prioritize classroom size, student-teacher ratios, direct student services, literacy and math curricula, teacher support, and special education.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes transparent budgeting
stanceCards.detail: Advocated transparent, collaborative budget decision-making with town boards, teachers, parents, and community members.

stanceCards.label: Links device policy to student well-being
stanceCards.detail: Argued phone-free districts report better focus and improved mental health.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Mnayarji described herself as an economist, entrepreneur, and Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member. She said she was running to address funding, curriculum, and culture challenges, specifically citing literacy reform, larger curriculum review, intentional technology use, and school culture.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on budget management
evidence.summary: She called for data-driven resource allocation, seeking grants and partnerships, and transparent, collaborative decision-making while protecting educational quality.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on curriculum changes
evidence.summary: She supported collaborative curriculum review beyond math and literacy, with data analysis, pilots, stakeholder feedback, classroom investment, and professional development.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on budget priorities
evidence.summary: She said top priorities should be classroom size, student-teacher ratios, direct student services, literacy and math curricula, teacher support, and special education, with administrative efficiencies preferred over instructional cuts.

evidence.title: Forum support for phone-free or device-free schools
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Mnayarji said she supported a phone-free experience and wanted to expand that to device-free language, arguing it would help focus and mental health.

evidence.title: 2025 official election results
evidence.summary: Official town results show Mnayarji received 1,964 votes in the School Committee race, finishing ahead of Christian Nixon and Karen Maruyama Bolognese in a contest for two seats.
